entries.name: Benzodiazepines (BDZ)
entries.memo: They **modulate** GABA-A (they do not open it on their own): the tetrad anxiolysis-hypnosis-muscle relaxation-anticonvulsant. Pitfall: **dependence**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **positive allosteric modulators** of the **GABA-A** receptor; ↑ **FREQUENCY of opening** of the Cl⁻ channel (**only in the presence of GABA**). Extracellular binding site (ECD), between the α and γ subunits (γ2 the most abundant).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: GABA-A receptor (BDZ site). **Subunit selectivity → effects**: **α1** = sedation/hypnosis (BZ1/ω1) + amnesia; **α2** = anxiolytic + muscle relaxant; **α3** = muscle relaxant; **α5** = amnesia. BZ3 (peripheral) → stimulates neurosteroids.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: oral (complete absorption), im (variable), **IV** (emergency/anaesthesia), rectal (children). High protein binding; **high Vd** (lipophilic; ↑ in the obese/elderly); they cross the placenta/milk. **Two-compartment** kinetics: **T/2α** (distribution, 2-10h) and **T/2β** (elimination, 2-100h). **Phase I** metabolism (oxidation → **active** metabolites) + **phase II** (glucuronoconjugation → inactive metabolites). **Classes by T/2β**: ① **intermediate-long** (nitro-BDZ: flunitrazepam, clonazepam, nitrazepam; **pronordiazepam-like**: diazepam → active metabolites, accumulation → ⚠️ not advised in the elderly/hepatopathic patient); ② **short** (**oxazepam-like**: lorazepam, oxazepam, temazepam → direct conjugation, **no active metabolites/accumulation** → preferred in the elderly); ③ **triazolo-BDZ** (alprazolam ~8h hypnoprotective, triazolam 3-4h hypnoinductive, midazolam).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: anxiety, insomnia, **alcohol withdrawal**, **status epilepticus**, premedication/balanced anaesthesia, sedation for procedures, central **spasticity**. **NOT analgesics** (only adjuvants in pain).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: therapeutic doses (sedation, asthenia, ↓ psychomotor performance, hangover, **anterograde amnesia** — "date rape drug"); toxic doses (orthostatic hypotension, hypothermia, confusion; ⚠️ **lethal with other CNS depressants**); the elderly (confusion, ataxia, **paradoxical effects**); ⚠️ **pregnancy** (teratogenic, neonatal withdrawal).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: ⚠️ **tolerance** (GABA-A down-regulation; marked for hypnosis/anticonvulsant, **not** for the anxiolytic effect); ⚠️ **dependence** (withdrawal 15-44%: ANS hyperactivity, anxiety, tremors, convulsions; more severe with short half-life/high intrinsic activity). Therapy **max ~6 days**; discontinuation **always tapered** (rebound).

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ⚠️ **NEVER with alcohol** (synergy → depression of the vital centres); **+ opioids → apnoea/death**; metabolic inhibitors (cimetidine, SSRI, propranolol, oral contraceptives, erythromycin, atorvastatin) → ↑ half-life of the active metabolites.

entries.name: Flumazenil
entries.memo: **Antagonist** of the BDZ site: antidote for overdose, but short half-life → the benzodiazepine can **re-enter**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **competitive antagonist** of the BDZ receptor.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: GABA-A receptor (BDZ site).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: **IV only**; rapid distribution (1-2 min); hepatic metabolism; low protein binding; **half-life ~1h** (effect 60 min-2h). Dose 0.1-0.2 mg repeated up to 3 mg (titration).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: **antidote for BDZ overdose**.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: nausea/vomiting, agitation, convulsions, hypotension/arrhythmia, panic attacks.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: short half-life → possible resedation.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: N/A specific.

entries.name: Barbiturates (phenobarbital, secobarbital, pentobarbital)
entries.memo: **They open the GABA-A channel on their own** → no ceiling effect → lethal in overdose. For this reason supplanted by the BDZs.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: modulators of **GABA-A**; ↑ **DURATION of opening** of the Cl⁻ channel (≠ frequency of the BDZs); the α and β subunits suffice (not γ). They potentiate the binding of GABA and of the BDZs. Ancillary: blockade of **Na⁺** channels (anaesthesia) and **K⁺** channels (high doses), **AMPA** antagonism → ↓ excitatory transmission.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: GABA-A receptor (barbiturate site) + Na/K channels + AMPA.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: N/A details in the documents.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: antiepileptics, **pharmacological coma** (head trauma → puts the electrical activity at rest), anaesthesia.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: ⚠️ **low therapeutic index** (linear dose-coma risk); ⚠️ **they increase pain perception** (NOT analgesics → contraindicated in pain); lethal synergy with alcohol.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: narrow therapeutic window.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ⚠️ **NEVER with alcohol** (synergy → depression of the vital centres).

entries.name: Z-compounds (zaleplon, zolpidem)
entries.memo: GABA-A **α1-selective**: hypnotics only, no anxiolysis; signature ADR = **sleepwalking**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **BDZ-like**, they act on a site **near** the BDZ site of GABA-A; **selective hypnotic** effect (no anxiolytic, no generalized sedation).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: GABA-A receptor (site near the BDZ site).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: N/A details in the documents.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: **insomnia** (they revolutionized sleep therapy).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: reduced compared to the BDZs; **no physical dependence, no withdrawal**.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: better safety profile than the BDZs.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: N/A specific.

entries.name: Etifoxine
entries.memo: **Non-BDZ** anxiolytic (via TSPO/neurosteroids): less dependence, but **hepatotoxicity**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **non-benzodiazepine** compound; allosteric modulator of GABA-A; it binds the **translocator protein (TSPO)** → ↑ synthesis of neurosteroids → modulates GABA in a physiological manner. Pleiotropic activity.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: GABA-A + TSPO.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: N/A details in the documents.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: anxiolytic (non-inferior to lorazepam); ⚠️ inhibition of neuroinflammation, **suppression of neuropathic pain** → **adjuvant in pain therapy**.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: no dependence/withdrawal/rebound anxiety on discontinuation.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: N/A specific.

entries.name: Buspirone and serotonergics (chronic anxiety)
entries.memo: Partial **5-HT1A** agonist: no dependence or sedation, but it acts **after 2-4 weeks** → only chronic GAD.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **Buspirone** = partial agonist of the post-synaptic **5-HT1A** receptor (acts in the presence of serotonin). **SSRI** → ↑ serotonin in the cleft → **5-HT1A down-regulation** → ↓ anxiety; ↑ **allopregnanolone** (positive allosteric modulator of GABA). *(Distinction: acute anxiety → GABA/BDZ; chronic anxiety → serotonin.)*

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: 5-HT1A receptor; serotonin transporter (SSRI).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: long-term administration; **delayed** anxiolytic effect (≠ rapid effect of the BDZs).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: **chronic anxiety**; SSRI in anxiety disorders associated with depression.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: SSRI → stimulation of 5-HT3/4/7 (sexual disturbances), 5-HT2A/2C (agitation).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: long onset latency.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: see file B3 (antidepressants).

entries.name: Neurosteroids (neuroactive steroids)
entries.memo: **Endogenous GABA-A modulators**; IV brexanolone treats **postpartum depression**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: produced in the CNS from **progesterone** (allopregnanolone, dehydroepiandrosterone, pregnenolone); **positive allosteric modulators** of GABA-A.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: GABA-A receptor (TMD site).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: N/A details in the documents.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: anxiolytic, anticonvulsant, sedative effect; target of drugs that modulate their synthesis (etifoxine, SSRI).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: deficiency → ↑ anxious state.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: SSRI ↑ allopregnanolone.

entries.name: General anaesthetics — principles
entries.memo: Anaesthesia = **three goals together**: hypnosis + analgesia + muscle relaxation. A single drug is not enough.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: potentiation of **GABAergic** transmission + ↓ **glutamatergic** transmission (and nicotinic ACh). Central sites: thalamus, reticular formation, cortex, hippocampus.

segments.label: Triad of anaesthesia
segments.value: abolition of consciousness, **analgesia**, muscle relaxation.

segments.label: Stages
segments.value: I = analgesia/sensory; **II = excitation/cortical irritation** (critical passage, must be extremely rapid — slow onset → disinhibition/aggressiveness); III = surgical anaesthesia (muscle relaxation); IV = **bulbar paralysis/death** (depression of the respiratory/vasomotor centre).

segments.label: Pre-anaesthesia
segments.value: barbiturates, **BDZ** (midazolam → amnesia/sedation pre-endoscopy), **anticholinergics** (scopolamine → ↓ secretions), muscle relaxants, **opioids** (fentanyl → analgesia), **anti-H2** (ranitidine → ↓ reflux), **antiemetics** (5-HT3 antagonists; D2 antagonists droperidol/metoclopramide — NOT domperidone, peripheral).

segments.label: Muscle relaxants (induction)
segments.value: nicotinic blockers → **vecuronium/atracurium** (competitive non-depolarizing), **succinylcholine** (tubocurarine obsolete). **Baclofen** = **GABA-B** agonist (analgesic + central muscle relaxant).

segments.label: Extra-CNS effects / ADR
segments.value: heart (⚠️ sensitization to catecholamines → arrhythmias, especially halogenated agents), hypothermia, nausea/vomiting (CTZ), **hepatotoxicity**. ⚠️ Caesarean section: **no diazepam**. Awakening = redistribution from the site of action (lipophilic anaesthetics).

entries.name: Inhalational / volatile anaesthetics (halothane, isoflurane, desflurane, sevoflurane, nitrous oxide, enflurane)
entries.memo: Potency measured in **MAC**; they modulate GABA/NMDA. Rare but fatal emergency: **malignant hyperthermia** → dantrolene.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: potentiation of GABA-A, ↓ glutamate; halothane-sensitive K⁺ channels (**TREK**, two-pore).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: GABA-A; TREK channels.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: **MAC** (minimum alveolar concentration — lower = more potent; halothane **0.8** vs nitrous oxide **100**); **blood/gas coefficient** (low = rapid onset/recovery; halothane 2.3 slow vs nitrous oxide 0.4 rapid); high oil/gas coefficient = crosses the BBB. Onset also influenced by cardiac output/perfusion.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: maintenance of anaesthesia. **Desflurane/sevoflurane** = more advanced (no respiratory irritation, rapid onset/recovery; desflurane requires a vaporizer); **nitrous oxide** = rapid but **incomplete anaesthesia** (no relaxation → + muscle relaxant).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: **halothane** (⚠️ arrhythmogenic, sensitizes the heart to catecholamines; **hepatotoxic**); **enflurane** (⚠️ epileptiform-like convulsions); **isoflurane** (⚠️ myocardial ischaemia in patients with coronary artery disease, respiratory irritation).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: halothane now superseded (toxicity).

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: nitrous oxide + muscle relaxant (for the missing relaxation).

entries.name: Intravenous anaesthetics (thiopental, propofol, etomidate, ketamine, fentanyl)
entries.memo: **Propofol** = rapid and clean induction; **ketamine** = dissociative NMDA-antagonist and bronchodilator.
freetext: **Thiopental** (barbiturate): potent anaesthetic, **immediate onset/recovery** (lipophilic) → manageable; ↓ cerebral metabolism (ischaemia risk in predisposed patients); ↓ respiration; can cause **laryngospasm**; **NO analgesia/relaxation**. (Other barbiturates not used in anaesthesia: long duration.)
**Propofol**: GABA-mimetic (barbiturate/BDZ-like); anaesthetic + poor analgesia; **very high manageability, the most widely used**; ↓ cerebral metabolism.

**Ketamine**: **NMDA antagonist** + ↑ outward K⁺ current (hyperpolarization); anaesthetic (veterinary/children, safe); ⚠️ **dissociative anaesthesia**, dysphoria/hallucinations; today a **rapid antidepressant** (SSRI add-on).

entries.name: Local anaesthetics — mechanism and pharmacokinetics
entries.memo: They block the **Na⁺ channel from the inside** → no action potential; in **acidosis/abscess** they work less.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: they block the **voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** (segment 6 of domains I, III, IV) → they stabilize the **inactivated state** → ↓ generation/propagation of the impulse. ⚠️ **USE-DEPENDENCE** (they reach the site with the channel open). **Not analgesics**.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: voltage-gated Na⁺ channel.

segments.label: Structure
segments.value: lipophilic pole + hydrophilic pole with an **ESTER** bond (procaine, tetracaine, cocaine, benzocaine → metabolism by **plasma esterases**, short duration, more hypersensitivity → topical) or **AMIDE** bond (lidocaine, articaine, mepivacaine, prilocaine, bupivacaine, ropivacaine → **hepatic metabolism**, longer duration; binding to **α1-acid glycoprotein**).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: the **non-ionized** form crosses the membranes, the **protonated** form binds the channel → ⚠️ **acidic pH (inflammation) → ineffective anaesthesia**. **Onset** ~ pKa (procaine pKa 9 → 20 min; lidocaine ~7.4 → 4 min). **Potency** ~ lipophilicity (too lipophilic → sequestration). **Duration** ~ protein binding (bupivacaine 96% → 10h).

segments.label: Target fibres
segments.value:
unmyelinated **C** fibres (pain/temperature → most sensitive), A-δ (myelinated), **A-α motor** (less sensitive — last blocked, first to recover). For myelinated fibres 3-5 covered nodes are required.
• **Intrinsic vasodilatory activity** (via NO) → ↑ systemic absorption, ↓ duration, ↑ ADR (lidocaine high; mepivacaine/articaine lower).

entries.name: Local anaesthetics — individual molecules
entries.memo: **Esters** (1 'i' in the name, cholinesterases, more allergies) vs **amides** (2 'i', hepatic metabolism, safer).
freetext: **Lidocaine** (amino-amide): rapid onset, intermediate duration, ⚠️ significant intrinsic vasodilation → rapid absorption/ADR; hepatic metabolism; spray for surface anaesthesia.
**Articaine** (amino-amide + ester group): among the most rapid; dual metabolism (hepatic + esterases) → short duration; **dental use**.
**Mepivacaine**: intermediate duration; ⚠️ **NOT in labour analgesia** (protonation in the neonate → neonatal toxicity); infiltrations/nerve blocks.
**Prilocaine**: lower vasodilation, high Vd; ⚠️ toxic metabolite **ortho-toluidine → methaemoglobinaemia**; dental use.
**Bupivacaine**: high lipophilicity → high potency; extensive protein binding → long duration (10h); used in **labour**; hepatic metabolism (CYP3A4); ⚠️ more significant **cardiac toxicity**.
**Ropivacaine** (amide): **less cardiotoxic** (S enantiomer); preferential block of sensory fibres; epidural/regional anaesthesia, duration > bupivacaine.
**Cocaine** (historical): first Na⁺ channel blocker; abandoned due to toxicity.

entries.name: Local anaesthetics — ADR and association with vasoconstrictor
entries.nameShort: Local anaesthetics — ADR and association with vasoconstrictor
entries.memo: Toxicity **first CNS then cardiac** (bupivacaine the worst); with **adrenaline** they last longer, **never in terminal districts**.

segments.label: ADR (from systemic absorption → Na⁺ channels in CNS/heart/muscle)
segments.value:
• **CNS** (lower doses, appear first): low doses → tinnitus, taste/vision alterations; higher doses → euphoria (↓ inhibitory interneurons) → drowsiness → **depression of the respiratory centre → death**.
• **Cardiovascular** (higher doses): initial tachycardia (vasodilation → reflex) → conduction block → **asystole**. Most frequent cause: **accidental intravascular injection**.
• **Haematological:** **methaemoglobinaemia** (prilocaine, lidocaine, benzocaine → o-toluidine).
• Idiosyncrasy (1st exposure), hypersensitivity (anaphylaxis, histamine release); local tissue damage at high doses.
• **Association with vasoconstrictor** (adrenaline, α1): ↓ systemic absorption, ↓ ADR, **↑ duration**, ↓ bleeding. ⚠️ adrenaline → systemic effects (hypertension, tachycardia, fibrillation); slows wound healing (not in regions with poor collateral circulation); ⚠️ patients on **non-selective β-blockers** (propranolol) → blood pressure rise/cardiac arrest (no problem with **metoprolol** cardioselective).

segments.label: Contraindications
segments.value: absolute = **pregnancy**; relative = dysionias/electrolyte alterations, contraindications to vasoconstrictors.

entries.name: Antidepressants — pathophysiological basis
entries.memo: ↑ monoamines within days, but the clinical effect arrives **after 2-4 weeks** (synaptic plasticity is required).

segments.label: Catecholaminergic hypothesis
segments.value: **reserpine** blocks the vesicular transporter → ↓ catecholamines → experimental model of depression (Brodie).

segments.label: Receptor theory
segments.value: ↓ serotonin → receptor up-regulation. **5-HT** ↔ feeding, sleep-wake, sexuality, impulsivity, OCD (5-HT2A); **NA** (Locus Coeruleus) ↔ energy.

segments.label: Key receptor post-synaptic 5-HT1A
segments.value: **silent/uncoupled from Gi → depression**; over-excited → anxiety.

segments.label: NA ↔ 5-HT "entangled"
segments.value: **α1** (Gq) ↑ firing/synthesis; **α2** (Gi) = autoreceptor (LC → ↓ NA release) or heteroreceptor (serotonergic terminal → ↓ 5-HT release).

segments.label: Onset latency
segments.value: **3-4 weeks** (initial side effects from up-regulation). Only rapid one (hours): **esketamine/ketamine**.

entries.name: Tricyclic antidepressants (TCA)
entries.memo: They block SERT+NET but also M1/H1/α1; dangerous overdose: the **3 Cs — Coma, Convulsions, Cardiotoxicity** (wide QRS).

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: inhibition of the reuptake carriers of **NA and 5-HT**. **Tertiary amines** (amitriptyline, imipramine, doxepin, clomipramine → NA+5-HT, broad); **secondary amines** (nortriptyline, desipramine, amoxapine → predominantly NA).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: NA/5-HT transporters; **α1, M1, H1** antagonism; blockade of Na⁺/Ca²⁺ channels.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: N/A details in the documents.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: depression (effective but **not first-line**); **adjuvants in pain** (they potentiate the descending inhibitory NA/5-HT pathways) — **NOT analgesics**.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: anti-M1 (blurred vision, ↓ digestion, constipation), anti-α1 (**orthostatic hypotension**), dizziness, sexual disturbances, **weight gain** (anti-H1); 5-HT2 up-regulation → hallucinations; ⚠️ blockade of Na/Ca channels → **cardiotoxicity** (arrhythmias, AV block); ADR last 6-7 weeks.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: ⚠️ contraindicated in arrhythmias/AV block/bundle branch block and with β-blockers.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ⚠️ **serotonin syndrome** with other serotonergics.

entries.name: MAOI (monoamine oxidase inhibitors)
entries.memo: They block MAO → **hypertensive crisis from tyramine** (cheeses, wine): the classic 'cheese effect'.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: inhibition of **MAO-A** (MAO-B → dopamine/Parkinson). 1st generation = **irreversible** inhibitors; **moclobemide** = reversible (can be discontinued).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: MAO-A.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: N/A details in the documents.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: depression (1st generation no longer used today).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: ⚠️ atropine-like effects; ⚠️ **cheese effect** (the **tyramine** in cheeses is not degraded → severe hypertensive crises).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: serious interactions → limited use.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ⚠️ serious dietary (tyramine) and pharmacological interactions.

entries.name: Serotonin syndrome
entries.memo: Too much 5-HT: **altered mental state + autonomic hyperactivity + clonus**. **Clonus** distinguishes it from NMS. Antidote: cyproheptadine.

segments.label: Cause
segments.value: **simultaneous intake of multiple serotonergic drugs** (e.g. TCA + SSRI + buspirone).

segments.label: Clinical picture
segments.value: agitation, tremor, sweating, muscle contractions, GI disturbances; ⚠️ **coma and death**.

segments.label: Prevention
segments.value: do not combine a second serotonergic (e.g. an analgesic) with a TCA used as an adjuvant.

entries.name: SSRI (citalopram, escitalopram, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, sertraline, paroxetine)
entries.memo: They block the **SERT**: first-line for depression/anxiety. Signature ADR = **sexual dysfunction**; gradual discontinuation.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **potent and selective inhibition of serotonin reuptake** (SERT). Sequence: ↓ somatodendritic reuptake → ↑ 5-HT → stimulates 5-HT1A autoreceptors → **down-regulation** of autoreceptors → ↑ firing → ↑ axonal release; after 5-HT2A down-regulation, the **post-synaptic 5-HT1A** (prefrontal cortex) signals → improvement.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: SERT (and 5-HT1A indirectly).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: latency **3-4 weeks**; ⚠️ many are **metabolic inhibitors** (fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, sertraline → CYP).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: depression (**FIRST LINE**, manageable), anxiety, OCD, eating disorders (anorexia, binge eating). **Peculiarities**: paroxetine/fluoxetine (also ↓ NA reuptake → for asthenic patients); sertraline (↓ DAT → ↓ anhedonia); fluvoxamine (adenosine antagonist); **citalopram** (the most selective → choice in **chronic anxiety**).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: akathisia (5-HT2 via nigrostriatal pathway → ↓ dopamine), OCD, anxiety, ↑ appetite/bulimia, nausea/vomiting (5-HT3), diarrhoea/cramps (5-HT4), ↓ libido. **NO** weight gain/anti-M1/anti-α1 effects (≠ TCA).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: long latency.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ⚠️ with β-blockers (propranolol) and contraceptives; ⚠️ serotonin syndrome.

entries.name: Reboxetine (NRI — noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor)
entries.memo: **Pure noradrenaline** (NET): for depression with psychomotor retardation and apathy.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: selective inhibitor of **noradrenaline** reuptake.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: NET.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: N/A details in the documents.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: depression with **noradrenergic** symptomatology (fatigue, apathy, psychomotor/cognitive retardation, attention deficit).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: ⚠️ not indicated in hypertensive patients/rhythm disturbances/cardiocirculatory risk.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: related: **atomoxetine** (NRI) → paediatric ADHD (≠ dextroamphetamines, which cause dependence).

entries.name: Bupropion (DRI — dopamine reuptake inhibitor)
entries.memo: **Dopamine + noradrenaline, zero serotonin** → no sexual dysfunction; helps with **smoking cessation** (↓ seizure threshold).

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: selective inhibitor of **dopamine** reuptake (+ ↑ NA).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: DAT.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: N/A details in the documents.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: **nicotine dependence** (↑ mesolimbic dopamine → ↓ craving); cocaine addicts; antidepressant effect not potent.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: ⚠️ ↑ NA → cardiovascular risk in smokers.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: related: **varenicline** (partial nicotinic α4β2 agonist) — today preferred for nicotine dependence.

entries.name: Venlafaxine (SNRI)
entries.memo: Dose-dependent: low = SSRI, high = **+ noradrenaline** (↑ blood pressure); useful in neuropathic pain.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: inhibitor of the reuptake of **serotonin and noradrenaline** (first 5-HT, then NA). "Synthesis between citalopram and reboxetine"; like a secondary amine **without** anti-α1/M1/H1 effects.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: SERT + NET.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: **does not interact with the cytochromes** → very manageable.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: **SECOND LINE** depression (resistance to SSRIs).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: better profile than the TCAs (N/A specific in the documents).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: second-line drug.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: N/A specific.

entries.name: NaSSA (mirtazapine)
entries.memo: **Mirtazapine**: its 'side effects' — sleep and appetite — are the **therapy** for depression with insomnia and weight loss.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **α2 antagonist** (auto- and hetero-receptors) → ↑ release of NA and 5-HT (↑ serotonergic firing via α1); antagonist of **5-HT3** (antiemetic), **5-HT2A**, **5-HT2C**, **H1**. The released 5-HT binds predominantly to 5-HT1.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: administer **before sleep** (sedation).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: depression; useful in **depressed males with sexual dysfunction** (5-HT2A blockade).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: ⚠️ ↑ NA harmful in cardiac patients (angina, vasospasm); anti-H1 → **weight gain + sedation**.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: N/A specific.

entries.name: SARI (trazodone, nefazodone)
entries.memo: **Trazodone**: at low doses a hypnotic without dependence; rare but serious: **priapism**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: strong **5-HT2A inhibition** (> mirtazapine) → they potentiate 5-HT1; + inhibition of serotonin reuptake. More **rapid** action (they directly block 5-HT2A).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: 5-HT2A; SERT.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: N/A details in the documents.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: depression.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: N/A specific in the documents.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: peculiarities: **nefazodone** (also ↓ NA reuptake); **trazodone** (anti-H1).

entries.name: Bipolar disorder — basis
entries.memo: **Mood stabilizers** continuously; the antidepressant given alone risks a **manic switch**.

segments.label: Picture
segments.value: alternation of **manic/hypomanic and depressive** episodes (also mixed episode); seasonal cyclicity; ↓ executive functions/decision making, sleep disturbances; ⚠️ ~¼ of patients attempt suicide.

segments.label: Etiopathogenesis
segments.value:
alteration of the circadian rhythm, catecholaminergic/serotonergic dysregulation, **↑ GSK3β** (glycogen synthase kinase-3β → pharmacological target).
• ⚠️ **Do not treat with antidepressants alone**: in the manic phase there is a risk of **psychotic switch**. Biological basis different from depression.

entries.name: Lithium (lithium salts)
entries.memo: Gold standard, but **very narrow therapeutic window**: tremor, polyuria, hypothyroidism, teratogenic (Ebstein's anomaly).

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: **GOLD STANDARD** of bipolar disorder; "mood/membrane stabilizer" (competition with **Na⁺**). Mechanism not fully known: it alters the **G proteins** and the coupling of the signal with membrane phospholipids/**IP3** → depresses transduction; **blocks GSK3β** → ↑ neuroprotection, ↓ neuroinflammation.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: G proteins / IP3; GSK3β.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: ⚠️ **narrow therapeutic window** → constant monitoring of **LITHAEMIA** (checks every 2-3 weeks; more frequent if renal deficit). **Lithium carbonate** (classic, extensive literature) vs **lithium orotate** (greater BBB permeability/lipophilicity → lower doses, lower toxicity/better compliance; but possible greater renal toxicity, few data).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: bipolar disorder — **prevention of relapses and cycling**; effective in **acute mania/hypomania** (≠ antidepressants).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: toxicity (competition with Na⁺) **renal, thyroid** (hypo-/hyper-), **cardiac** (conduction), neural. Severe in the long term (~10 years): **cognitive impairment, nephrotoxicity, hypothyroidism, hyperparathyroidism**. ⚠️ pregnancy/puerperium can worsen it.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: narrow therapeutic window, critical compliance.

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: N/A specific.

entries.name: Valproic acid (sodium valproate) — as a mood stabilizer
entries.memo: Effective stabilizer but a **potent teratogen** → never in a woman of childbearing potential without safe contraception.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: blockade of **Na⁺** channels (membrane stabilizer) + blockade of **Ca²⁺** channels (↓ glutamate) + **NMDA** blockade (neuroprotection) + **↑ GABA** (↑ GAD, ↓ GABA transaminase, ↓ reuptake) = GABA-mimetic.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: Na/Ca channels, NMDA, GABA system.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: ⚠️ **metabolic inhibitor** (like some SSRIs).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: bipolar disorder (undisputed mood stabilizer); epilepsy (see B9).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: ⚠️ significant **TERATOGENIC** effect (severe malformations, especially in the 1st trimester).

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ⚠️ metabolic inhibitor → with **atorvastatin** → **rhabdomyolysis**; with anticoagulants/antiplatelet agents → haemorrhagic effect (except clopidogrel = no event).

entries.name: Carbamazepine — as a mood stabilizer
entries.memo: It blocks Na⁺ channels and is an **autoinducer** (CYP); risk of **Stevens-Johnson** (HLA-B*1502) and hyponatraemia.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: blockade of **Na⁺** channels (membrane/mood stabilizer) + **↑ GABAergic activity**.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: Na⁺ channels; GABA.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: ⚠️ **metabolic INDUCER** (↑ CYP synthesis) — like phenytoin.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: bipolar disorder; epilepsy (see B9); **trigeminal neuralgia** (analgesic).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: N/A specific in the documents (see B9).

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ⚠️ inducer → ↓ efficacy of co-administered drugs (e.g. anticoagulants/antiplatelet agents → thrombotic risk).

entries.name: Lamotrigine
entries.memo: The stabilizer of the **depressive pole** of bipolar disorder; **very slow titration** or **rash up to SJS**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: a reworking of carbamazepine **without its pharmacokinetic effects** (induction); significant blockade of **Na⁺** channels.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: Na⁺ channels.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: absence of the inducing effect of carbamazepine.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: bipolar disorder; epilepsy (see B9).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: N/A specific in the documents (see B9).

segments.label: Pharmacological Associations / Adjuvants
segments.value: N/A specific.
